title: Declaring typed arrays
instruction:
TypeScript infers the element type of an array from its initial values. Once inferred, only matching values can be added.

Create arrays of different types and explore their behavior.
successMessage: Each array has a locked element type. Trying to push a `string` into a `number[]` is a compile-time error. This prevents a whole class of runtime bugs.

title: Array of objects — typed collections
instruction:
When an array holds objects, TypeScript enforces the shape of every element. This is one of the most common patterns in real applications.

Build a typed collection of users.
successMessage: TypeScript knows every element of `users` is a `User`. Inside `.filter()` and `.map()`, TypeScript infers `u` is `User` automatically — giving you autocomplete and type checking in callbacks.

title: map — transform every element
instruction:
`.map()` transforms an array, creating a new array of the same length. TypeScript infers the return array's type from your callback's return type.

Transform a price list into formatted strings.
successMessage: `.map()` returns `{ label: string; display: string; annual: string }[]` — TypeScript infers the shape from the callback. You don't need to annotate it.

title: filter — select matching elements
instruction:
`.filter()` returns a new array containing only elements where the callback returns `true`. TypeScript preserves the element type in the filtered array.

Filter orders by status and calculate totals.
successMessage: `paid` has type `Order[]` — the filter doesn't change the element type. If you use a type guard in the filter callback, TypeScript can narrow the element type of the result.

title: reduce — accumulate a result
instruction:
`.reduce()` accumulates a result by running a callback for each element. The accumulator type must be annotated when it differs from the element type.

Group orders by customer using reduce.
successMessage: The `<Record<number, number>>` annotation tells TypeScript what type the accumulator starts as. Without it, TypeScript would infer `{}` (empty object type) and the type of the accumulator would be wrong.

title: find and findIndex — locate elements
instruction:
`.find()` returns the first matching element (or `undefined`). TypeScript knows the return type is `T | undefined`, forcing you to handle the not-found case.

Look up users by ID and email.
successMessage: `find()` returns `User | undefined`. TypeScript forces you to handle `undefined` — that's why `?.` and `??` are needed. This prevents runtime errors when an element isn't found.

title: sort — sort in place with a comparator
instruction:
`.sort()` sorts an array in place. For non-string arrays, you must provide a comparator function. TypeScript ensures the comparator returns a number.

Sort users by name and by score.
successMessage: `[...players]` creates a copy before sorting — `.sort()` mutates in place. The comparator `(a, b) => b.score - a.score` sorts descending. Positive result = b comes first.

title: flat and flatMap — working with nested arrays
instruction:
`.flat()` flattens nested arrays. `.flatMap()` combines map and flat in one step. Both are useful for working with API responses that contain nested data.

Flatten a list of categories with their items.
successMessage: `.flatMap()` maps then flattens one level — cleaner than `.map().flat()`. TypeScript infers the result type from the callback return type.

title: Tuples — fixed-length typed arrays
instruction:
A tuple is an array where the length and type of each position are fixed. TypeScript enforces both the length and the type at each index.

Use tuples to represent coordinates and key-value pairs.
successMessage: Tuples are destructured by position. `const [lat, lng] = location` gives `lat` and `lng` both type `number`. Position 0 and 1 of `Point2D` are both `number`, so both are correctly typed.

title: Readonly arrays — immutable collections
instruction:
`readonly T[]` prevents mutation — no push, pop, splice, or sort. Use it for constants and data that shouldn't change.

Create immutable configuration arrays.
successMessage: `readonly string[]` is assignable to `string[]` but not vice versa. Functions that only need to read can accept `readonly T[]`, ensuring they don't accidentally mutate their input.

title: Array spread and rest — combining arrays
instruction:
The spread operator `...` unpacks an array. It's used to combine arrays, pass array elements as arguments, and create copies.

Combine and split arrays using spread.
expectedOutput: Full stack (7): HTML, CSS, JavaScript, TypeScript, Node.js, Python, Go
All skills (8): HTML, CSS, JavaScript, Node.js, Python, Go, Docker, Kubernetes
Updated frontend: HTML, CSS, JavaScript, TypeScript, React
successMessage: Spread creates new arrays without mutating the originals. TypeScript infers the combined array type — if arrays have different element types, TypeScript creates a union type array.

title: every and some — testing array conditions
instruction:
`.every()` returns true if ALL elements pass the test. `.some()` returns true if ANY element passes. Both short-circuit for performance.

Validate a batch of form inputs.
successMessage: `.every()` checked all required fields — they're all filled, so the form is valid. `.some()` found the empty bio field. Both are often cleaner than manual loops.

title: Type guards with arrays — narrowing element types
instruction:
When an array contains a union type, you can use type guards inside array methods to narrow the type and work safely with specific variants.

Process a mixed array of different event types.
successMessage: The `(e): e is LoginEvent` return type annotation is a **type predicate** — it tells TypeScript that when the filter callback returns `true`, the element is `LoginEvent`. After filtering, `logins` is `LoginEvent[]`, giving you access to `.userId`.
